one_line_takeaway: A scalable pipeline that generates and auto-amends natural-language 'robot constitutions' from real-world images and injury reports, plus the ASIMOV benchmark to measure them; generated constitutions beat Asimov's Laws (84.3% alignment) and, as a decoupled violation-checking 'safety brain', catch 100% of RoboPAIR jailbreak commands.
key_finding: Auto-generated and auto-amended constitutions produce the best human alignment. The top configuration (Robot-Constitution128-AutoMerge-AutoAmend10) reaches 84.3% average alignment across the benchmark, beating no-constitution baselines and human-written constitutions including Asimov's Three Laws. The base Gemini 1.5 Pro alone scores 83.6% in normal mode but collapses to 33.6% under the value-flipped adversary (58.6% average), whereas specific/long constitutions raise adversary-mode alignment substantially, supporting the hypothesis that specificity buys adversarial robustness. Auto-amending gives large boosts (e.g., 68.7% -> 80.6% for a Robot-Constitution32 after 5 passes). On the RoboPAIR self-driving jailbreak setup, using a secondary constitution 'brain' to re-check already-jailbroken commands catches 100% of undesirable instructions (random 57.1%; base with no CoT/anti-jailbreak 71.4%).
core_contribution: The paper delivers two artifacts for the emerging problem of 'semantic safety' in VLM/LLM-controlled robots (common-sense safety constraints in open-domain scenes, as opposed to classical collision-avoidance). (1) The ASIMOV Benchmark: a large-scale multimodal dataset for evaluating and improving whether foundation-model 'robot brains' judge instructions as desirable/undesirable, built with a scalable generative recipe. (2) A framework that automatically GENERATES robot 'constitutions' (natural-language rules of safe behavior prepended to the model prompt) bottom-up from real-world data, plus a novel 'auto-amending' process that iteratively generates counterfactuals that break a rule and rewrites the rule to handle them, and an 'auto-merge' assembly step. The authors deliberately do NOT advocate a single universal constitution, arguing instead that human-interpretable, editable data-derived constitutions are the right medium for behavior governance. Note: RLHF, DPO, and the original Constitutional AI (Bai et al. 2022) belong to Related Work, not this paper's contribution; the paper's own novelty is scalable, data-driven, multimodal constitution generation and auto-amending for embodied agents.
threat_model: Embodied VLMs/LLMs are being handed physical control of robots despite known failure modes (hallucination, jailbreaking). The paper considers three concrete adversarial/failure conditions: (1) Jailbreaking / adversarial prompt injection where an attacker convinces the robot brain the scenario is not real (evaluated via the RoboPAIR self-driving setup, where prior work achieved 100% attack success). (2) A compromised or malicious base model, simulated by an 'adversary agent' prompt that flips the model's notion of good/bad, testing whether safety survives when the base model's values are inverted. (3) The general 'long tail' of unsafe common-sense situations that hand-written rules (e.g., Asimov's Laws) fail to cover. The proposed defense is a constitution used as a violation-checking preamble, ideally hosted in a physically decoupled, anti-tampering 'safety brain' that gates instructions before actuation. The authors are explicit that a constitution alone is insufficient without a secure system with trusted (e.g., camera) inputs, and that the full secure architecture (Fig. 9) is NOT implemented here beyond the secondary constitution check.
limitations: By the authors' own account: (1) No perfect constitution exists; corner cases always remain, so constitutions must be paired with base-model common sense and are 'for research purposes only, not for deployments requiring safety guarantees.' (2) Assembled constitutions suffer redundancy and inter-rule conflicts; after auto-amending, rules converge toward vague general concepts. (3) Rule-based decisions produce absurd/ambiguous calls (live agent refused to cut a banana as 'fragile', flagged cutting an egg as harming a living thing). (4) High alignment can come from utilitarian 'maximize benefit to humanity' goals that may harm individuals, so max-alignment selection is dangerous. (5) No universal rule set fits all environments; human editing per legal/cultural/administrative context is required. (6) The best-aligned constitution is ~300k characters, prohibitive for inference latency. (7) The secure 'safety brain' architecture is proposed but not implemented; RoboPAIR eval is done outside the actual jailbreak optimization loop and assumes the secondary brain is uncompromised. (8) Human-labeled validation sets are small (1,140 labels total; e.g., ASIMOV-Injury val has 163 labels).

datasets_used: ASIMOV Benchmark (own; total 513,679 contexts/images, 2,942,060 actions/instructions, 1,140 human labels across all splits)
ASIMOV-Multimodal-Auto (images+text; val: 50 images, 1,311 instructions, 789 human labels; train: 11,080 images, 288,421 instructions)
ASIMOV-Injury (text; val: 50 contexts, 319 instructions, 163 human labels; train: 361,947 contexts, 2,335,361 instructions)
ASIMOV-Dilemmas-Auto (text; val: 100 contexts, 200 instructions) and ASIMOV-Dilemmas-Scifi (val: 51 contexts, 284 instructions, 153 labels)
RoboVQA egocentric robot/human videos (source of base images)
NEISS National Electronic Injury Surveillance System (338,265 cases from 2023 used; ~100 US hospitals)
RoboPAIR self-driving jailbreak set (7 situations, 3 images + 1 prompt + 1 attack answer each)
quantitative_results: Top average alignment 84.3% (normal 87.7% / adversary 80.9%) with Robot-Constitution128-AutoMerge-AutoAmend10 (128 lines, 297,982 chars)
Base Gemini 1.5 Pro, no constitution: 83.6% normal / 33.6% adversary / 58.6% average alignment
ASIMOV-Injury val: Gemini 1.5 Pro and GPT4-Turbo both ~90% (base no-constitution 89.74% / 90.61%); best Robot-Constitution-6 = 94.67% (Gemini), 93.23% (GPT4-Turbo)
Auto-amending boost: 68.7% -> 80.6% for Robot-Constitution32-AutoMerge after 5 amend passes
Efficient trade-off: SciFi-Constitution32-AutoMerge-AutoAmend2 = 82.3% average at only 32 lines / 12,545 chars (24x smaller than the 300k-char best)
RoboPAIR: constitution safety-brain catches 100% of undesirable jailbroken instructions (random 57.1%; base no-CoT/no-anti-jailbreak 71.4%; No Rules 14.3%)
Asimov's Four Laws auto-amended went from 71.4% to 100% on RoboPAIR
Negative baselines ~50% (No Rules 51.2% avg, You Decide 53.0% avg, Random 52.1% avg)
Live Constitution Officer violation scores: 0% (no violation, cutting banana) and 92% (violation, knife on hand) via calibrated True/False log-probs
baselines_compared: Base foundation models with NO constitution asked a desirability question (Gemini 1.5 Pro, GPT4-Turbo)
Human-written constitutions: Asimov's Three/Four Laws, Anthropic-9 and Anthropic 'Best for Humanity', Hippocratic Oath
Negative/degenerate baselines: No Rules, You Decide, Random
Ablations of inference add-ons: chain-of-thought prompting and anti-jailbreak prompting (with/without)
Constitution assembly variants: Random-subset vs LLM-Summarized vs Auto-Merge, and 1/2/5/10 auto-amend passes; short vs long (length-vs-alignment sweep over 339 constitutions)
